summary: Given-When-Then is a structured approach to writing tests developed by Daniel Terhorst-North and Chris Matts as part of Behavior-Driven Development (BDD). The pattern breaks tests into three sections: Given (pre-conditions), When (the behavior being tested), and Then (expected outcomes). While popularized through Cucumber and BDD, the pattern is essentially a reformulation of existing testing conventions like the Four-Phase Test and Arrange-Act-Assert. The approach works for both formal business-facing tests and informal unit test comments.
key_points: Given-When-Then divides tests into pre-conditions (Given), behavior (When), and expected changes (Then)
The pattern is a reformulation of the Four-Phase Test (Setup, Exercise, Verify, Teardown) and Arrange-Act-Assert conventions
While associated with BDD and Cucumber, the style can be applied to any kind of test including unit tests
The 'given' section describes state but frameworks interpret it as commands to establish that state
Teardown is omitted from BDD style because it doesn't add to the communication aspect of specification by example
money_quotes: Given-When-Then is a style of representing tests - or as its advocates would say - specifying a system's behavior using SpecificationByExample.
The given part describes the state of the world before you begin the behavior you're specifying in this scenario.
Since we're talking about using examples as specifications, it makes sense to show this with an example.
You can use the Given-When-Then style with any kind of tests.
I've characterized the given as a description of the pre-condition state because that's the way I prefer to think of it.
key_insight: Given-When-Then is not unique to BDD but rather a communication-focused reformulation of established test structuring patterns that can be applied across any testing context.
